coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescence-based colorimetric sensor for the portable analysis of antioxidants via smartphone
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Qian
Li, Yang
Wei, Wei
Huang, Juan
Lu, Dai
Liu, Shigang
Shi, Xingbo
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a ratiometric fluorescence-based colorimetric sensor was developed for the portable analysis of antioxidant capacity (AOC) using a smartphone. The sensor used carbon dots (CDs) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as fluorescence signal substrate and manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) as a fluorescence quencher, a catalyst of the substrate. Without a target, MnO2 NSs effectively quench the fluorescence of the CDs via inner filter effect and could catalyze the oxidization of nonfluorescent OPD, forming a yellow fluorescent product (ox-OPD). With a target, MnO2 NSs were destructed into Mn2+, resulting in the loss of the fluorescence quencher and catalytic function. The fluorescence of the CDs was recovered, and the fluorescence of the ox-OPD decreased. The fluorescence color change of the system from yellow to blue was further digitized using a smartphone via the red–green–blue (RGB) analysis. Choosing glutathione as the proof-of-concept target, the two readout modes of the fluorescence spectra and RGB analysis achieved the detection limits of 1.84 and 8.33 μM, respectively. Ratiometric fluorescence exhibited precise and reliable detection owing to the built-in self-calibration function, while the colorimetric readout via a smartphone realized the portable determination. This proposed strategy shows a great potential application in assessing the AOC of food samples and controlling food quality.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antioxidants
Ratiometric fluorescence
Colorimetric
Manganese dioxide nanosheets
Carbon dots
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the CDs and MnO2 NSs
Ratiometric fluorescence-based colorimetric sensor for detecting GSH, AA, and GA
Mechanism of the ratiometric fluorescence- based colorimetric sensor
Characterization of CDs and MnO2 NSs
Properties of the ratiometric fluorescence- based colorimetric sensor
Investigation on the practicability of real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the CDs and MnO2 NSs. Fluorescence (A) and FTIR (B) spectra of the CDs; TEM image and histogram (C) of the CDs; (D) UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 NSs; (E) TEM image, Inset: TEM image of MnO2 NSs after 100 times dilution; (F) FTIR spectrum of MnO2 NSs.

Feasibility experiments based on GSH. (A) Fluorescence spectra of 1: MnO2 NSs, 2: OPD, 3: MnO2 NSs + OPD, 4: MnO2 NSs + OPD + GSH system; (B) Images of samples 1–4 obtained using a smartphone; (C) Fluorescence spectra of ox-OPD; (D) TEM images of MnO2 NSs before and after adding of GSH; (E) The fluorescence lifetime of CDs and CDs with MnO2 NSs; (F) Fluorescence spectra of 5: CDs, 6: MnO2 NSs, 7: OPD, 8: CDs + MnO2 NSs, 9: CDs + MnO2 NSs + OPD, 10: CDs + MnO2 NSs + GSH + OPD system; (G) Images of samples 5–10 obtained using a smartphone.
Optimization tests. The analysis of the concentrations of MnO2 NSs (A) and OPD (B); The optimization of the incubation temperature (C) and incubation time (D).

GSH detection using the ratiometric fluorescence-based colorimetric sensor. (A) Fluorescence spectra of the detection system at different GSH concentrations; (B) Relationship between the concentration of GSH and the I420/I560 value, inset: the corresponding fitting curve of I420/I560 value versus the varying concentration of GSH (4 –100 μM); (C) Images of the solutions at different GSH concentrations under UV lamp, blue channel, green channel, and red channel; (D) The relationship between the concentration of GSH and B/(G + R) value, inset: the corresponding fitting curve of B/(G + R) value versus the varying concentration of GSH (10–60 μM).

AOC analysis of lemons using the ratiometric fluorescence-based colorimetric sensor and DPPH method (A) Correlation analysis between the fluorescence signal and DPPH methods; (B) Correlation analysis between the RGB signal and DPPH method.
Working principle of the ratiometric fluorescence-based colorimetric sensor for the portable detection of antioxidants using a smartphone.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescence-based colorimetric sensor for the portable analysis of antioxidants via smartphone

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Micro & Nano Biosensing Technology in Food Safety, Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Science and Biotechnology, College of Food Science and Technology, Hunan Agricultural University, Changsha 410128, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: & Nano Biosensing Technology in Food Safety
Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Science and Biotechnology
College of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Micro & Nano Biosensing Technology in Food Safety, Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Science and Biotechnology, College of Food Science and Technology, Hunan Agricultural University, Changsha 410128, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Testing Technology Company of Changsha Research Institute of Mining and Metallurgy Co., LTD., Changsha 410012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Testing Technology Company of Changsha Research Institute of Mining and Metallurgy Co., LTD.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Testing Technology Company of Changsha Research Institute of Mining and Metallurgy Co., LTD., Changsha 410012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a ratiometric fluorescence-based colorimetric sensor was developed for the portable analysis of antioxidant capacity (AOC) using a smartphone. The sensor used carbon dots (CDs) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as fluorescence signal substrate and manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) as a fluorescence quencher, a catalyst of the substrate. Without a target, MnO2 NSs effectively quench the fluorescence of the CDs via inner filter effect and could catalyze the oxidization of nonfluorescent OPD, forming a yellow fluorescent product (ox-OPD). With a target, MnO2 NSs were destructed into Mn2+, resulting in the loss of the fluorescence quencher and catalytic function. The fluorescence of the CDs was recovered, and the fluorescence of the ox-OPD decreased. The fluorescence color change of the system from yellow to blue was further digitized using a smartphone via the red–green–blue (RGB) analysis. Choosing glutathione as the proof-of-concept target, the two readout modes of the fluorescence spectra and RGB analysis achieved the detection limits of 1.84 and 8.33 μM, respectively. Ratiometric fluorescence exhibited precise and reliable detection owing to the built-in self-calibration function, while the colorimetric readout via a smartphone realized the portable determination. This proposed strategy shows a great potential application in assessing the AOC of food samples and controlling food quality.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antioxidants, as potent free radical scavengers, provide a positive effect on cell response and immune function [1,2]. Therefore, increasing attention has been devoted to them in nutrition and medicine because they particularly prevent oxidative stress-related diseases by moderating free-radical concentrations [3–6]. Recently, antioxidant capacity (AOC) has been used to detect antioxidants in foods or biological materials [7,8]. Spectrophotometry assays are widely used for the AOC determination, such as electron-transfer (ET)-based cupric-ion reducing antioxidants capacity (CUPRAC) [9,10], ferric reducing antioxidant power (FRAP) [11,12], hydrogen atom transfer (HAT)-based oxygen radical absorbance capacity (ORAC) [13,14], and mixed 2,2 diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) assays [15,16]. Nevertheless, the major drawbacks of those methods, such as harsh conditions, complicated procedures, and lengthy analysis time, triggering the development of new approaches for a simple, portable, and sensitive determination of AOC determination.

Nanomaterial-based methods, such as electrochemistry [17], colorimetric [18] and fluorescent [19], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [20] and Molecular Imprinting [21–23], have been used to determine small molecules. Among these, fluorescence analysis is mature and robust in biosensing, and it is highly sensitive and compatible with analytical platforms [24]. However, most of the fluorescence sensing studies have been confined to analyze single-emission fluorescence, which is susceptible to interference from background signals, environmental perturbations, and instrumental conditions [25,26].

Thus, ratiometric fluorescence bioassays are preferred for precise and reliable detections due to the dual-emission fluorescent signal output mode [27,28]. It is competent for conquering signal interference from instruments and microenvironment owing to its built-in self-calibration function. Notably, the ratiometric fluorescence platforms exhibit significant fluorescence-color variation [29,30], enabling further portable visual identification.

The advancements in nanomaterials create new techniques for establishing ratiometric fluorescence sensors. Carbon dots (CDs) are a super choice for signal output owing to their strong photobleaching resistance, good chemical stability, low biotoxicity, and high fluorescence [31–33]. As one kind of redox-active layered transition-metal dioxide, manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) with large surface areas exhibit excellent physicochemical properties. Due to the oxidase-like activity, MnO2 NSs can catalyze the conversion of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) into oxide (ox-OPD) with a strong fluorescence peak at 560 nm [34,35]. Particularly, the broad absorption spectrum endows MnO2 NSs with a strong fluorescence-quenching ability via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or internal filtration effect (IFE) mechanism [36,37]. Based on the above considerations, integrating of the fluorescence characteristics of CDs and the functional MnO2 NSs into one biosensor provides a promising opportunity for the advanced development of ratiometric fluorescence assays.

Recently, smartphone-based analysis has been widely applied in biological and chemical sensing because of its powerful computing ability and high-resolution camera [38,39]. The fluorescence-color variation exhibited by the ratiometric fluorescence can be effectively analyzed using a smartphone. The color variation captured by the smartphone can be rapidly read by red–greenblue (RGB) color images with the help of software, such as Image J. Hence, the cooperation of the ratiometric fluorescence strategy and smartphone-based analysis introduces a desirable choice for portable, cost-effective, and simple on-site detection [40–42].

In this study, a portable ratiometric fluorescent-based colorimetric biosensor for AOC determination was established and validated, exemplified by glutathione (GSH), ascorbic acid (AA), and gallic acid (GA) (Scheme 1 ). Functional MnO2 NSs played the roles as the fluorescence quencher, the catalyst of the substrate. CDs and OPD were the fluorescence signal substrates. CDs was anchored on the surface of MnO2 NSs, followed by the fluorescence quenching at 420 nm via IFE. Without a target, MnO2 NSs catalyzed the conversion of OPD into ox-OPD, producing an emission peak at 560 nm [43]. However, with a target, MnO2 NSs was reduced to Mn2+, losing the oxidase-like activity. The fluorescence of CDs was recovered, and no ox-OPD was generated (expressed as the fluorescence peak decreasing at 560 nm and increasing at 420 nm). Correspondingly, the fluorescence color was changed from yellow to blue, as analyzed using a smartphone coupled with the RGB profile. This ratiometric fluorescence signal makes the detection results more reliable and corrects signal perturbations. The ratiometric fluorescence-based colorimetric signal obtained using a smartphone enables visual and portable determination. Overall, our investigations establish the significant potentiality of this sensor as a robust tool for AOC determinations and practical applications.

Potassium permanganate (KMnO4), sodium dodecyl sulfate, ethanol, potassium chloride (KCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), Glycine (Gly), Glutamic acid (Glu), Proline (Pro), serine (Ser) and AA were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). Sulfuric acid was purchased from Xingkong Chemical Glass (Zhuzhou, China). OPD, Lysine (Lys), Threonine (Thr), glucose and GSH were acquired from Sigma Aldrich (USA). Lemon was purchased from a local supermarket. Naringin and GA were obtained from the Alfa Aesar Chemical Company (Beijing, China). The Milli-Q water (18.2 MΩ·cm−1) from the Millipore system was used throughout the experiment.

The morphologies and sizes of the CDs and MnO2 NSs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, Japan). The CD and MnO2 nanosheet solutions were placed dropwise on a carbon-coated copper grid and dried at room temperature for the TEM analysis. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured using a Shimadzu UV-2600 double-beam UV–vis spectrophotometer with a 1 cm quartz cell (Purkinje General Instrument Co. Ltd., Beijing, China). Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer using a 2.5/5 nm slit width and equipped with a 1 cm quartz cell (Hitachi, Kyoto, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (Shimadzu, IR Affinity-1) using KBr pellets, scanning from 4000 to 500 cm−1 at room temperature. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was conducted on a Nano-ZS90 Zetasizer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). The fluorescence lifetime was characterized by Steady-state/Lifetime Spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh FLS1000, UK). Photographs were collected using an iPhone 13 smartphone with autofocus (Apple, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the CDs and MnO2 NSs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs was synthesized by hydrothermal method using naringin as the precursor. Briefly, 0.0116 g naringin was dissolved in 5 mL of ethanol solution and ultrasonicated for 10 min. Afterward, transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained yellow solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the precipitation and converted into water solution by rotary evaporation. Finally, the CDs solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and freeze-dried to obtain solid powder for further experiments.

MnO2 NSs were synthesized via a redox reaction using potassium permanganate as a precursor [44]. In a typical synthesis, 5 mL of 0.1 mol/L sodium dodecyl sulfate solution was added into 250 μL of 0.1 mol/L sulfuric acid solution, and thereafter, the above mixture was mixed with 44.25 mL of water solution, heated at 95 °C, and stirred for 15 min. Subsequently, 500 μL of 0.05 mol/L potassium permanganate solution was added and stirred at 95 °C for 60 min, after which the solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min; afterward, a brown-black precipitate was obtained. Following purification, the concentration of MnO2 NSs was evaluated by its absorption value at 380 nm (ε = 9.6 × 103 L/moL/cm).

The ratiometric fluorescence intensity at 420 nm and 560 nm (I420/I560) was employed to evaluate the stepwise experiments. First, the fluorescence spectrum of CDs (0.5 mg/mL, 200 μL), MnO2 NSs (154 μM, 100 μL), and OPD (50 mM, 50 μL) were investigated in a system made up to 500 uL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution. Second, the role of CDs, MnO2 NSs, and GSH in the detection system was explored using the mixture of MnO2 NSs (154 μM, 100 μL) and OPD (50 μL 50 mM) in 200 μL of PBS; the mixture of MnO2 NSs (154 μM, 100 μL), OPD (50 μL 50 mM), and GSH (50 μL 10 mM) in 200 μL of PBS; the mixture of CDs (0.5 mg/mL, 200 μL) and MnO2 NSs (154 μM, 100 μL) in 200 μL of PBS; the mixture of CDs (0.5 mg/mL, 200 μL), MnO2 NSs (154 μM, 100 μL), and OPD (50 μL 50 mM) in 150 μL of PBS; the mixture of CDs (0.5 mg/mL, 200 μL), MnO2 NSs (154 μM, 100 μL), OPD (50 μL 50 mM), and GSH (50 μL, 10 mM) in 200 μL of PBS. Thereafter, the ratiometric fluorescence intensity of all the systems was measured after a incubation for 10 min at 50 °C.

Moreover, the mixture samples were excited under a 360 nm UV lamp, and thereafter, a series of color photos of fluorescence were obtained using a smartphone. Image J was employed to analyze the information of the fluorescence photos into blue (B), green (G), and red (R) channel photos via the split channel menu commands. B/(G + R) ratio was estimated to explore the feasibility of portable RGB detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence-based colorimetric sensor for detecting GSH, AA, and GA
ce:section.ce:para: Various concentrations of GSH (50 μL, final concentration of 0.001–1 mM) were added into the mixture solution (200 μL of 0.5 mg/mL CDs and 100 μL of 100 μM MnO2 NSs) and incubated for 10 min at room temperature. Afterward, 50 μL of 75 mM OPD solution and 100 μL of PBS buffer solution were added and further incubated for 10 min at 50 °C in the dark. The fluorescence intensity ratio of I420/I560 was monitored using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The B/(G + R) value was evaluated after excitation for 15 min via a 360 nm UV lamp using a smartphone. The procedure for detecting AA (final concentration, 0.01–1 mM) and GA (final concentration, 0.01–1 mM) was the same as that for GSH.

The selective experiments were operated by adding target GSH (100 μM), AA (100 μM), GA (100 μM) and other interfering substances, such as metal ions (1 mM, Na+, K+, Ca2+), amino acids (1 mM, Gly, Glu, Lys, Pro, Thr, Ser) and glucose (1 mM). The assay procedure was the same as presented above. The stability of the sensor was evaluated at different storage time (1–18 d) by using detection GSH (100 μM) as an example.

Eighty gram of peeled and mashed lemon was added to 10 mL anhydrous ethanol solution, and the solution was ultrasonicated for 10 min at room temperature to improve the extraction efficiency. The resulting extraction solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane. The purified extract was preserved at − 20 °C and used within one week. The proposed ratiometric fluorescence-based colorimetric sensor was used for antioxidant determination in lemons.

DPPH method was employed as a standard for comparison. Typically, 100 μL of DPPH solution (1 mM) was mixed with 400 μL of the lemon extract. Then, the mixture was fully shaken and incubated for 30 min in the dark. The free-radical scavenging rate was monitored using a microplate reader and calculated according to the formula below:
Free
radical
scavenging
rate
%
=
A 0
-
A 1
/
A 1
× 100 .
A0 is the absorbance value of the blank experiment at 517 nm, and A1 is the absorbance value of the experimental sample at 517 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the ratiometric fluorescence- based colorimetric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrates the principle of the sensor for the portable detection of antioxidants, selecting CDs and OPD as the fluorescence signal substrates. MnO2 NSs, as the fluorescence quencher, quenched the blue fluorescence of the CDs at 420 nm via the IFE effect. Without a target, the MnO2 NSs with strong oxidase-like activity catalyzed the conversion of OPD into ox-OPD. Therefore, a conspicuous emission peak at 560 nm attributed to ox-OPD was observed. With a target, MnO2 NSs was reduced to Mn2+, resulting in the loss of the fluorescence quencher and catalytic functions. The decomposition of MnO2 NSs triggered the recovery of the blue fluorescence of CDs and the decrease in ox-OPD fluorescence. Following the target increase, the fluorescence peak at 560 nm decreased and the peak at 420 nm increased. Fluorescence intensity of the system was detected using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with I420/I560 as one signal, photographed using a smartphone for RGB analysis with B/(R + G) as the other signal. Thus, a ratiometric sensor with two readout modes of fluorescence and colorimetric analysis was prepared for the sensitive and portable detection of antioxidants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and MnO2 NSs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs was hydrothermally generated using naringin as the precursor and characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy, FTIR spectroscopy, DLS, and TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the CDs exhibited strong blue fluorescence, and the emission peak was located at 420 nm under the excitation of 360 nm with a fluorescent quantum yield (QY) of 28.33%. The FTIR spectrum of the CDs was investigated to identify the surface functional groups (Fig. 1B). The absorption peaks at 3414–3441 cm−1, 1641 cm−1, 1396 cm−1, and 1068 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of –OH, –C = O, C–O–C, and C–OH groups [45], respectively. the TEM and DLS (Fig. 1C) showed that the CDs were spherically shaped with a size of 1.09 nm and considerably dispersed. Furthermore, UV–vis spectroscopy, FTIR, and TEM were performed to verify the preparation of MnO2 NSs. Fig. 1D shows that the MnO2 NSs have a wide UV–vis absorption spectrum in the range of 200–700 nm with an absorption peak at 380 nm. According to the Lamberts Law, the concentration of MnO2 NSs was calculated to be 636 μM with a absorption coefficient ε of 9.6 × 103 L/mol/cm [46]. Additionally, MnO2 NSs displayed a typical two-dimensional morphology with lamellar structure in the TEM image dilution ratios (Fig. 1E). The FTIR characteristic peaks of the MnO2 NSs located at 555 cm−1, 1639 cm−1, and 3410 cm−1 were stemmed from the stretching vibrations of Mn–O, the bending vibrations of the combination of O–H and Mn atoms, and the stretching vibrations of O–H [47], respectively. These results confirmed the successful synthesis of the CDs and MnO2 NSs.

The feasibility of the sensor for detecting antioxidants was confirmed through several control experiments, targeted by GSH. First, the oxidase-like activity of the MnO2 NSs was evaluated. As displayed in Fig. 2 A, although no significant fluorescence was observed for MnO2 NSs or OPD, the mixture of MnO2 NSs and OPD presented a strong fluorescence emission at 560 nm. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of ox-OPD proved the successful oxidation of OPD by MnO2 NSs, ascribed to the consistent emission peak (Fig. 2C). After adding GSH into the mixture, the fluorescence generated at 560 nm was significantly decreased. Comparing the TEM images (Fig. 2D) of MnO2 NSs before and after adding of GSH, it was observed that the MnO2 NSs with the ultrathin and transparent lamellar structure had been decomposed, making it unfeasible to oxidize the OPD to ox-OPD. The above results corroborated that MnO2 NSs possessed pronounced oxidase-like activity and the function of target identification. The high fluorescence generated by ox-OPD was regulated by GSH.

Additionally, this study evaluated the detection of GSH. CDs was anchored on the surface of MnO2 NSs by the electrostatic adsorption. Subsequently, the high fluorescence at 420 nm of CDs was quenched (Fig. 2F). From Fig S1, the zeta potential of the MnO2 NSs was electropositive, while the CDs was negative. The alteration of the zeta potential of their complex demonstrated that the CDs has been attached to the surface of the MnO2 NSs. To demonstrate the quenching mechanism of CDs fluorescence by MnO2 NSs. The fluorescence lifetime variation, fluorescence spectrum of CDs, UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 NSs and CDs were collected. As shown in Fig. 2E, there was no influence on the fluorescence lifetime of CDs upon the addition of MnO2 NSs. Thus, we inferred that the quenching mechanism could not be FRET, dynamic quenching or photo-induced electron transfer (PET), whose fluorescence quenching will be accompanied by variation in fluorescence lifetime [48]. In addition, no characteristic peaks appeared of the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs with the addition of MnO2 NSs proved that the quenching mechanism was not static quenching (Fig. S2B) [49]. Additionally, the wide UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 NSs considerably overlapped with the emission spectra of CDs (Fig. S2A), resulting in the IFE between MnO2 NSs and CDs.

Therefore, MnO2 NSs were indeed used as fluorescence quenchers. Investigations also revealed that OPD addition triggered an appearance of new ox-OPD peaks at 560 nm. However, after adding GSH into the MnO2 NSs/CDs/OPD system, MnO2 NSs transformed into Mn2+, restoring the fluorescence of CDs and quenching the fluorescence of ox-OPD (Fig. 2F). Subsequently, the colorimetric RGB verification was performed. The photos shown in Fig. 2B and G were taken under a UV lamp using a smartphone. Color fluorescence images were handled by Image J. As all characterizations were consistent with the results in Fig. 2A, F, the ratiometric fluorescence–based colorimetric sensor was clearly and successfully validated.

Finally, the effects of target (GSH, AA, GA) with reducibility on ox-OPD were investigated as shown in Fig S3. And the results illustrated that the target with reducibility cannot directly reduce ox-OPD and affect its fluorescence, resulting in a change in the ratio fluorescence signal.

The concentration of MnO2 NSs and OPD, incubation temperature, and time were optimized. On the one hand, few MnO2 NSs will affect the sensitivity of the sensor; on the other hand, more MnO2 NSs will affect the stability of the detection. With the increase of MnO2 NSs concentration (including 1, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 μM), the fluorescent of CDs at 420 nm gradually decreased and the fluorescent of ox-OPD at 560 nm gradually increased (Fig. S4A and Fig. 3 A). A 20 μM MnO2 NS concentration yielded an equivalent fluorescence-change intensity at 420 nm and 560 nm (ΔI420 nm = I1(420 nm)–I0(420 nm), ΔI560 nm = I0(560 nm)–I1(560 nm), where I0 is the fluorescent value of the blank experiment, and I1 is the fluorescent value of the experimental sample), reaching a balance point of fluorescence quenching and recovery. Hence, we adopted the 20μM concentration in subsequent assays to ensure the stability. Subsequently, the influence of the OPD concentration was investigated (Fig. S4B and Fig. 3B). It was observed that although the ΔI560 nm value gradually increased with increasing concentration from 0 to 7.5 mM, it was almost near the plateau after 7.5 mM. Thus, 7.5 mM of OPD was selected as the optimal concertation.

Subsequently, the incubation temperature (20 °C, 40 °C, 50 °C, 60 °C, and 80 °C) and time (1, 5, 10, 15, and 20 min) was optimized. The value of ΔI560 nm progressively increased with the temperature but nearly reached a plateau after 50 °C (Fig. S4C and Fig. 3C). As shown in Fig. S4D and Fig. 3D, the ΔI560 nm value increased with a prolonged incubation time from 0 to 10 min. After 10 min, a negligible change in these control groups was observed. Therefore, in the following studies, the sensor was carried out at 50 °C for 10 min.

The effects of the pH for the determination of antioxidants were conducted. Firstly, the effect of the pH for the catalytic reaction of MnO2 NSs was evaluated (Fig.S5A). After the addition of OPD into MnO2 NSs under the pH of 2–13, ox-OPD exhibited different fluorescence intensity at 560 nm, but it is stable in the 3.0–10.0 range. Strong acid or alkali environment will affect its catalytic activity. Subsequently, the effect of the pH for CDs was conducted (Fig.S5B). Under the excitation at 360 nm, fluorescence intensity of CDs exhibited a pH-dependent property. A strongest fluorescence intensity was obtained at pH of 7. Finally, the effect of the pH for the target detection was investigated (Fig. S5C). After the addition of GSH, it was observed that the maximum I420/I560 value was obtained at pH of 7, whereas a higher or lower pH yielded the decreased I420/I560 value. To sum up, the pH of 7 was chosen for the determination of antioxidants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of the ratiometric fluorescence- based colorimetric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, the sensing ability of the sensor with ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric readout signals was assessed. Both fluorescent and colorimetric signals exhibited GSH concentration-dependent behavior. Various concentrations of GSH from 0.001 to 1 mM were measured to evaluate the sensitivity and linear analytic range. As indicated in Fig. 4 A, a typical sigmoidal dose–response plot was observed. The I420/I560 value progressively augmented with the increasing concentration of GSH and gradually stabilized (Fig. 4B). The inset manifested an excellent linear relationship by plotting the I420/I560 value against various concentrations of GSH from 4 −100 μM. The linear range was further verified (Fig. S6). The resultant calibration equation was I420/I560 = 0.16073c + 0.73477, with a linear regression coefficient (R2) of 0.98361. According to this equation and based on the calculation of 3σ/S (σ is the standard deviation for a blank solution and S is the slope of the calibration curve), the LOD is 1.84 μM. The color of the detection system continuously changed from yellow to blue under a 360 nm UV lamp, which was recognized using a smartphone (Fig. 4C). The color intensity of the B channel increased, while the color intensity of the G and R channels gradually decreased. A linear relationship (B/(G + R) = 0.00932c + 0.48562) between the B/(G + R) value and GSH concertation (10–60 μM) yielded a R2 of 0.97918 (Fig. 4D). The LOD is calculated to be 8.33 μM.

Additionally, AA and GA, as the extensively used antioxidants, were detected using the sensor. Fig. S7 displayed a linear relationship between the I420/I560 values and the concentrations of AA in the range of 0.01–1 mM, as descripted in equation of I420/I560 = 5.86482c + 0.7056. The LOD is determined to be 2.55 μM. Meanwhile, the B/(G + R) value increased with the different concentrations of AA from 0.06 to 0.8 mM. The regression equation was described as B/(G + R) = 0.29727c + 0.41813, with the LOD of 58.17 μM. For GA determination, the LOD is 1.97 μM via the fluorescent signals and 29.13 μM based on the colorimetric signals (Fig. S8). Details of calibration curve for LOD/limit of quantification (LOQ) calculation was shown in Table S1 and Table S2.

The specificity was studied to evaluate the interference of potential species in food sample, including metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+), amino acids (Gly, Glu, Lys, Pro, Thr, Ser) and glucose. There were negligible I420/I560 and B/(G + R) values changes in these control groups even their concentration are 10 times higher than that of target (Fig S9). In the presence of GSH, AA and GA, whether alone or in the presence of those with other interfering analytes, that remarkable I420/I560 and B/(G + R) values changes were observed. Therefore, the proposed sensor exhibits responsiveness and anti-interference performance toward antioxidants.

Stability study of the sensor was further investigated after varying storage periods (1–18 d) (Fig. S10). Fortunately, the I420/I560 and B/(G + R) values appear flat, indicating good stability of the sensor for the analysis of antioxidants, even after a long-term storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on the practicability of real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To test the applicability of the developed ratiometric fluorescence-based colorimetric sensor for AOC analysis in real samples, the antioxidants in lemon samples were monitored. Lemon juice was first collected and diluted in multiples before detection, followed by sample determination using the DPPH method. Fig. 5 shows that these methods exhibited good Pearson correlation coefficients of 0.90471 (fluorescence signal) and 0.9027 (colorimetric signal), demonstrating our sensor is a promising strategy for the sensitive and portable determination of antioxidants in real samples with a workable reality.

Herein, we constructed a ratiometric fluorescence-based colorimetric sensor for the portable analysis of AOC by taking advantage of the superior properties of functional MnO2 NSs. GSH, AA, and GA were exemplified for the proof-of-concept study of the sensor. Without a target, MnO2 NSs quenched the fluorescence of CDs via IFE and catalyzed the oxidization of non-fluorescent OPD to form a yellow fluorescent ox-OPD. However, in the presence of the target, MnO2 NSs was reduced into Mn2+, and CDs was released from the surface of the MnO2 NSs. The I420/I560 value was applied as a ratiometric fluorescence signal. The fluorescence color changed from yellow to blue under a UV lamp, which was further analyzed using a smartphone via Image J. The ratio of B/(G + R) was taken as the colorimetric signal. These two types of signal readout modes were target-dependent and cooperatively realized the sensitive and portable detection of antioxidants. Moreover, this sensor allowed AOC analysis in lemons with high Pearson correlation coefficients, compared with the DPPH method. After a successful proof-of-concept, we expect that this ratiometric fluorescence-based colorimetric sensor can be extended to detect various types of antioxidant-related analytes in more fields, contributing to AOC analysis studies.

Qian Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Yang Li: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Wei Wei: Methodology, Formal analysis. Juan Huang: Methodology, Visualization, Formal analysis. Dai Lu: Methodology, Visualization, Formal analysis. Shigang Liu: Validation, Formal analysis. Xingbo Shi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge that this work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31972155, 32001778), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2022JJ10028, 2020RC3050, 2020JJ5249), Key Research and Development Program of Hunan Province (2021NK2021), Training Program of Excellent Young Innovators of Changsha (kq2206036), Natural Science Foundation of Changsha (kq2014066).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108625.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
